alsoKnownAs: Island Apple Snail (P. maculata trade name)

nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwater habitats — lakes, ponds, rivers, swamps, irrigation channels, and floodplain wetlands. Generally found in shallow, well-vegetated margins with soft substrate and abundant submerged or floating macrophytes. Tolerates a wide thermal range but is restricted by sustained cold; the southernmost record is the Paso de las Piedras reservoir in southern Buenos Aires province, Argentina.
nativeRange.biotope: Neotropical lowland freshwater wetland / floodplain margin

decorPreferences: tightly sealed lid with a 10-15 cm air gap above the waterline — adults oviposit on emergent surfaces and will climb out of any open tank
calcium-rich water or cuttlebone supplementation to support shell growth
sand or fine gravel substrate (snails will burrow opportunistically)
robust or artificial plants only — Pomacea canaliculata is a voracious macrophyte grazer and will strip live aquatic plants
gentle filtration and moderate flow; avoid high-flow setups

diet.notes: The Apple Snail is an extremely broad feeder. Juveniles under about 1.5 cm feed primarily on algae and detritus, but adults shift heavily toward higher plants and will consume virtually any soft macrophyte in the tank. In an aquarium setting they readily accept blanched vegetables such as lettuce, cucumber, courgette, spinach, and carrot, as well as sinking algae wafers, snail or shrimp pellets, and detritus. Calcium supplementation — through cuttlebone or calcium-rich foods — is generally advised to support continuous shell growth. The species has also been documented opportunistically scavenging on dead fish and other animal protein.

goodWith: peaceful community fish that don't eat snails
shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) where competition for food is managed
other large peaceful snails (Nerite, Mystery Snail Pomacea diffusa)
avoidWith: snail-eating fish (loaches, large pufferfish, larger cichlids)
live planted display tanks — plants will be eaten
any setup where escape to local waterways is possible (legally restricted invasive species)

imageCandidates.notes: KENPEI, 3264x2448. Live snail in natural shallow-water habitat with the long siphon extended; globose olive shell with dark spiral banding, whole animal sharp and in frame.
FlyingBatt, 1080x1440. Live snail in water showing the yellow muscular foot and long paired cephalic tentacles; shell carries natural algal cover as seen on wild individuals.
FlyingBatt, 4032x3024. Whole live adult, algae-covered globose shell; illustrates typical wild rice-field specimen. Extra angle, out-of-water.

summary: Large, voracious South American apple snail and one of the world's worst invasive species — banned for sale or possession across the EU, UK, and the US. Included for completeness only; check local law before considering this species.
careNotes:
LEGAL STATUS — READ FIRST. Pomacea canaliculata is listed on the EU Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern list (Regulation 1143/2014), making sale, keeping, breeding, transport, and release illegal across all EU member states and Northern Ireland. The UK has equivalent restrictions under the Invasive Alien Species (Enforcement and Permitting) Order 2019. In the US the species is regulated as a Federal Noxious Weed and injurious agricultural pest under USDA APHIS, and many states — notably California, Texas, Hawaii, and Florida — explicitly prohibit possession; many keepers also cite a 2014 Lacey Act-related listing. Australia treats it as a prohibited import. Possession outside permitted research settings is generally illegal across major jurisdictions; this species is included here for identification and educational reference. Confirm local and state law before acquiring under any circumstances.

Husbandry context (where legal). Pomacea canaliculata is a large (often 8–15 cm shell), highly active freshwater snail native to the La Plata and lower Paraná basins of South America. It is an obligate air-breather with a long siphon and lays bright pink-to-orange egg clutches above the waterline — a tightly sealed lid with a 10–15 cm air gap is non-negotiable, both to allow breathing and to prevent escape. Calcium-rich, moderately hard water with pH 7.0–8.0 supports continuous shell growth; soft acidic water causes shell erosion. Temperatures of 20–28°C are generally advised; sustained exposure below approximately 18°C is reported to be lethal. The species is an extreme generalist feeder — adults will systematically destroy live aquarium plants, making planted-tank coexistence impractical. The species also carries Angiostrongylus cantonensis (rat lungworm) in parts of its range; hands should be washed after handling, and contact with eyes or mouth avoided. The closely related sister species Pomacea maculata (Island Apple Snail) is morphologically very similar, similarly invasive, and subject to the same regulatory and husbandry guidance in most jurisdictions; the two are frequently confused in the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: Sexes are separate. Females climb above the waterline and deposit conspicuous bright pink to orange egg clutches — typically 200 to 600 or more eggs — on emergent vegetation, rocks, glass, or any surface above the water; eggs hatch in roughly 2 weeks. The species is enormously prolific: a single mated female can produce hundreds of offspring in a season, which is the principal reason for its global invasive status. Where temperatures stay above approximately 22°C, reproduction tends to proceed readily, though breeding is illegal in most jurisdictions where this species can be kept at all. Where egg clutches do appear, pushing them below the waterline causes them to fail — a standard and humane control measure. Never release juveniles, adults, or egg clutches into local waterways under any circumstances.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Ampullariidae, Architaenioglossa), native range (Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay), adult shell size (up to 15 cm), polyphagous diet shifting from algae/detritus as juvenile to macrophytes as adult, above-waterline pink egg-laying behavior, invasive status (top 100 World's Worst Invasive Alien Species, 40th-worst in Europe), agricultural impact on rice/taro, and Angiostrongylus cantonensis disease-vector risk.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (slice-designated secondary)
additional.notes: Slice-designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; not usable. Fell back to alternate secondaries per playbook step 3.

additional.name: Apple Snail Website (applesnail.net) — Care
additional.notes: Backup secondary (specialist apple-snail husbandry site). Cross-checked water temperature (18–28°C optimal), calcium-rich water requirement, tank-size guidance (~10 L per mid-sized 5 cm snail — used as a floor; biased larger to 75 L for the typical 8–15 cm adult per playbook step 3 tank-size convention), copper sensitivity (highly sensitive — confirms molluscVariant.copperSensitive = true), above-waterline egg-laying with required 15 cm air gap, escape risk and lid requirement, and plant-eating risk (listed among species that devour aquarium vegetation).

additional.name: Aquadiction — Apple Snail (Pomacea canaliculata) Care Guide
additional.notes: Backup secondary. Cross-checked ~4-year typical lifespan (longer at cooler temperatures), 20°C lower thermal tolerance limit, omnivorous diet acceptance of blanched vegetables and prepared sinking foods, gentle-flow habitat preference, obligate air-breathing via siphon, secure-lid requirement, and incompatibility with snail-eating fish (loaches, pufferfish, larger cichlids).

additional.name: USGS NAS — Channeled Applesnail (Pomacea canaliculata) Fact Sheet
additional.notes: Regulatory/invasive cross-source. Confirmed native range (La Plata and Lower Paraná basins), US established populations (Arizona, California, Idaho, Hawaii, NE Florida, NC), aquarium-trade introduction pathway, rice-crop pest status, rat lungworm disease-vector role, and 'top 100 worst invasive species' designation. Basis (with EU 1143/2014 list) for hobbyistAdvisory legally-restricted flag.

additional.name: EU Regulation 1143/2014 — Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern
additional.notes: Legal source. Pomacea canaliculata is on the EU IAS Union list (added 2016 under this Regulation), making possession, sale, keeping, breeding, transport, and release illegal across all EU member states. Equivalent restrictions apply in the UK under the Invasive Alien Species (Enforcement and Permitting) Order 2019. Confirms 'legally-restricted' advisory level.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: EU/UK/USA Lacey Act + many jurisdiction bans on sale and possession due to invasive risk to rice paddies and aquatic ecosystems
